content.text: Semantic Kernel (SK) is an open-source SDK from Microsoft for integrating large language models into applications. Unlike LangChain's wide-ecosystem approach or CrewAI's role-based abstraction, SK is designed around the

— a central object that wires together an LLM service, a set of plugins (tools), and memory. The SDK supports Python, C#, and Java. In Python, SK follows the same pattern as other frameworks: tools are annotated functions, the kernel invokes the LLM, and auto-invocation handles the tool-calling loop.
: the central orchestrator — holds the LLM service, plugins, and settings
: a class whose methods become tools; each method is decorated with
: a callable tool derived from a plugin method; schema inferred from annotations and docstrings
: the LLM backend — OpenAI, Azure OpenAI, or any OpenAI-compatible endpoint
: controls when the kernel auto-invokes tools (
Holds services, plugins, and execution settings
Type-annotated function; schema inferred from docstring
OpenAI, Azure OpenAI, or compatible endpoint
Stateful agent with a thread (conversation history)
Vector search over stored text using embeddings
Plugins are the primary extension point in Semantic Kernel. A plugin is a plain Python class. Any method decorated with
becomes a tool. The method's type annotations determine the input schema; the docstring becomes the tool description the LLM sees. Use
map directly to the tool's JSON schema the LLM sees. If you omit them, SK uses the method name and its docstring.

lets you describe each parameter individually — the string becomes the parameter's description in the schema. This precision helps the model fill in arguments correctly, especially for optional parameters.
Building a Kernel and Running Completions
is the entry point for all SK operations. Add a chat completion service, register plugins, then run a completion. With
, the kernel handles the full tool-calling loop: it dispatches tool calls, collects results, and continues until the LLM produces a final answer.

import asyncio
from semantic_kernel import Kernel
from semantic_kernel.connectors.ai.open_ai import (
    OpenAIChatCompletion,
    OpenAIChatPromptExecutionSettings,
)
from semantic_kernel.connectors.ai.function_choice_behavior import FunctionChoiceBehavior
from semantic_kernel.contents import ChatHistory

async def main():
    kernel = Kernel()

    # Add the OpenAI chat service (reads OPENAI_API_KEY from env)
    kernel.add_service(
        OpenAIChatCompletion(service_id="chat", ai_model_id="gpt-4o")
    )

    # Register plugins as named collections of tools
    kernel.add_plugin(MathPlugin(), plugin_name="Math")
    kernel.add_plugin(WeatherPlugin(), plugin_name="Weather")

    # Auto: the kernel calls tools automatically until the LLM stops requesting them
    settings = OpenAIChatPromptExecutionSettings(
        service_id="chat",
        function_choice_behavior=FunctionChoiceBehavior.Auto(),
    )

    history = ChatHistory()
    history.add_user_message(
        "What is 2 ** 16? Also, what is the weather in Tokyo in fahrenheit?"
    )

    chat_service = kernel.get_service("chat")
    result = await chat_service.get_chat_message_content(
        chat_history=history,
        settings=settings,
        kernel=kernel,
    )
    print(result.content)
    # 2^16 is 65536. The weather in Tokyo is 71 degrees F.

asyncio.run(main())

, the kernel enters a loop: the LLM produces a tool-call request, the kernel dispatches it to the plugin method, appends the result to the history, and sends the updated conversation back to the LLM. This continues until the model returns a text response with no tool call.
forces at least one tool invocation;
disables tool invocation entirely (useful when you want the model to reason without using any tools).
For multi-turn conversations, Semantic Kernel provides
. It is an agent object with a name, instructions, and a kernel. It keeps conversation history in an
appends the new message and response to the thread, so the agent remembers earlier turns without the caller managing history manually.

import asyncio
from semantic_kernel.agents import ChatCompletionAgent, AgentThread
from semantic_kernel.connectors.ai.open_ai import OpenAIChatPromptExecutionSettings
from semantic_kernel.connectors.ai.function_choice_behavior import FunctionChoiceBehavior

async def main():
    kernel = Kernel()
    kernel.add_service(
        OpenAIChatCompletion(service_id="chat", ai_model_id="gpt-4o")
    )
    kernel.add_plugin(MathPlugin(), plugin_name="Math")
    kernel.add_plugin(WeatherPlugin(), plugin_name="Weather")

    settings = OpenAIChatPromptExecutionSettings(
        service_id="chat",
        function_choice_behavior=FunctionChoiceBehavior.Auto(),
    )

    agent = ChatCompletionAgent(
        service_id="chat",
        kernel=kernel,
        name="AssistantAgent",
        instructions=(
            "You are a helpful assistant with math and weather tools. "
            "Use tools to provide accurate answers."
        ),
        execution_settings=settings,
    )

    # AgentThread holds the conversation history across turns
    thread = AgentThread()

    # Turn 1
    async for message in agent.invoke(messages="What is 512 * 7?", thread=thread):
        print(f"Turn 1: {message.content}")
    # Turn 1: 512 times 7 is 3584.

    # Turn 2 — agent remembers the prior result
    async for message in agent.invoke(messages="Now double that result.", thread=thread):
        print(f"Turn 2: {message.content}")
    # Turn 2: Double 3584 is 7168.

asyncio.run(main())
accumulates the conversation history between calls. The second turn works correctly because the thread carries the prior result.
is an async generator that yields
items with partial text chunks that you can write to a websocket or SSE stream in a web application.
: a memory abstraction that stores text as vectors and retrieves the most relevant entries for a query. This is the building block for
: before answering a question, the agent searches memory for relevant documents and uses them as context.

import asyncio
from semantic_kernel import Kernel
from semantic_kernel.connectors.ai.open_ai import OpenAIChatCompletion, OpenAITextEmbedding
from semantic_kernel.memory import SemanticTextMemory
from semantic_kernel.connectors.memory.in_memory import InMemoryStore
from semantic_kernel.core_plugins import TextMemoryPlugin

async def main():
    kernel = Kernel()
    kernel.add_service(OpenAIChatCompletion(service_id="chat", ai_model_id="gpt-4o"))

    # Embedding service — converts text to vectors for semantic comparison
    embedding_service = OpenAITextEmbedding(
        service_id="embedding",
        ai_model_id="text-embedding-3-small",
    )
    kernel.add_service(embedding_service)

    # In-memory vector store (swap with Qdrant/Azure AI Search for production)
    memory = SemanticTextMemory(
        storage=InMemoryStore(),
        embeddings_generator=embedding_service,
    )

    # Index documents
    collection = "product-docs"
    await memory.save_information(collection, id="doc-1",
        text="The Pro plan supports up to 100 API calls per minute.")
    await memory.save_information(collection, id="doc-2",
        text="The Enterprise plan includes unlimited API calls and an SLA.")
    await memory.save_information(collection, id="doc-3",
        text="All plans include email support. Enterprise adds 24/7 phone support.")

    # Semantic search: cosine similarity against query embedding
    results = await memory.search(collection, "What are the API rate limits?", limit=2)
    for r in results:
        print(f"[relevance={r.relevance:.2f}] {r.text}")
    # [relevance=0.93] The Pro plan supports up to 100 API calls per minute.
    # [relevance=0.81] The Enterprise plan includes unlimited API calls and an SLA.

    # Register as a tool so the LLM can call memory.search during an agent run
    kernel.add_plugin(TextMemoryPlugin(memory), plugin_name="Memory")

asyncio.run(main())
as a tool. In an agent loop with
, the model learns to call the memory search tool to look up facts before answering. For production, swap
with a persistent vector database. SK ships connectors for Azure AI Search, Qdrant, Chroma, Weaviate, and Redis. The
API stays the same regardless of the backend.
Semantic Kernel occupies a distinct niche: it is the leading framework for Azure-integrated and multi-language teams, while other frameworks are more Python-centric:
framework of choice for Azure-integrated applications

: it has first-class Azure OpenAI support, integrates with Azure AI Search for memory, and the C# SDK lets .NET teams use the same patterns as the Python SDK. For pure-Python projects not tied to Azure, LangChain's wider tool ecosystem or the OpenAI Agents SDK's simpler surface area may be preferable. AutoGen remains strongest for code-generation loops; none of these frameworks is universally best — choose based on your cloud platform, team language, and whether built-in RAG is a requirement.
Beyond native Python functions, SK lets you define tools as
. A semantic function is a string template with

placeholders. When invoked, SK fills in the variables and sends the rendered prompt to the LLM. This is useful for reusable prompt patterns: summaries, classifications, translations, or structured extractions that you want to treat as first-class tools callable from the kernel.

import asyncio
from semantic_kernel import Kernel
from semantic_kernel.connectors.ai.open_ai import OpenAIChatCompletion
from semantic_kernel.prompt_template import PromptTemplateConfig
from semantic_kernel.functions import KernelArguments

async def main():
    kernel = Kernel()
    kernel.add_service(OpenAIChatCompletion(service_id="chat", ai_model_id="gpt-4o"))

    # Define a semantic function: a prompt template with variables
    summarize_config = PromptTemplateConfig(
        template=(
            "Summarize the following text in exactly {{$sentence_count}} sentences.\n\n"
            "TEXT:\n{{$input}}"
        ),
        name="summarize",
        description="Summarize a text into N sentences.",
        input_variables=[
            {"name": "input", "description": "The text to summarize", "is_required": True},
            {"name": "sentence_count", "description": "Number of summary sentences", "default": "2"},
        ],
    )

    # Register the semantic function as a plugin
    summarize_fn = kernel.add_function(
        plugin_name="TextTools",
        function_name="summarize",
        prompt_template_config=summarize_config,
    )

    # Invoke with KernelArguments (named variable substitution)
    text = (
        "Semantic Kernel is a Microsoft open-source SDK for integrating LLMs into applications. "
        "It supports Python, C#, and Java. The SDK uses plugins to define tools, "
        "a Kernel as the central orchestrator, and SemanticTextMemory for vector retrieval."
    )
    result = await kernel.invoke(
        summarize_fn,
        KernelArguments(input=text, sentence_count="1"),
    )
    print(result)
    # Semantic Kernel is a Microsoft open-source SDK that enables Python, C#, and Java
    # applications to integrate LLMs through plugins, a central Kernel, and vector memory.

asyncio.run(main())
KernelArguments), renders the prompt, and sends it to the LLM. This is SK's way of defining prompt-based tools alongside native Python tools: both appear in
and can be called the same way. A semantic function's output is whatever the LLM returns for that prompt — you do not write Python logic; the prompt
the logic. Combine native and semantic functions in the same plugin to mix computation with LLM reasoning.
Kernel Filters: Intercept and Observe Function Calls

— hooks that run before or after a kernel function (tool) is invoked. Use filters for cross-cutting concerns: logging, rate limiting, input sanitization, result caching, or usage metrics. Register a filter with
: it wraps every tool invocation in the kernel.

import asyncio, time
from typing import Callable, Any
from semantic_kernel import Kernel
from semantic_kernel.filters.functions.function_invocation_context import FunctionInvocationContext
from semantic_kernel.connectors.ai.open_ai import OpenAIChatCompletion

# A filter is an async function that wraps function invocation
async def logging_filter(
    context: FunctionInvocationContext,
    next: Callable[[FunctionInvocationContext], Any],
) -> None:
    plugin = context.function.plugin_name
    name = context.function.name
    args = context.arguments

    start = time.perf_counter()
    print(f"[CALL] {plugin}.{name} args={dict(args)}")

    # Call the actual function
    await next(context)

    elapsed = time.perf_counter() - start
    print(f"[DONE] {plugin}.{name} result={context.result} ({elapsed*1000:.0f}ms)")

# A filter that blocks tool calls with suspicious inputs
async def safety_filter(
    context: FunctionInvocationContext,
    next: Callable[[FunctionInvocationContext], Any],
) -> None:
    for key, value in context.arguments.items():
        if isinstance(value, str) and "DROP TABLE" in value.upper():
            from semantic_kernel.functions import FunctionResult
            context.result = FunctionResult(
                function=context.function.metadata,
                value="Error: unsafe input detected",
            )
            return  # do NOT call next() — the real function is skipped
    await next(context)

async def main():
    kernel = Kernel()
    kernel.add_service(OpenAIChatCompletion(service_id="chat", ai_model_id="gpt-4o"))
    kernel.add_plugin(MathPlugin(), plugin_name="Math")

    # Register filters — they run in registration order
    kernel.add_filter("function_invocation", logging_filter)
    kernel.add_filter("function_invocation", safety_filter)

    from semantic_kernel.connectors.ai.open_ai import OpenAIChatPromptExecutionSettings
    from semantic_kernel.connectors.ai.function_choice_behavior import FunctionChoiceBehavior
    from semantic_kernel.contents import ChatHistory

    settings = OpenAIChatPromptExecutionSettings(
        service_id="chat",
        function_choice_behavior=FunctionChoiceBehavior.Auto(),
    )
    history = ChatHistory()
    history.add_user_message("What is 3 ** 8?")

    chat_service = kernel.get_service("chat")
    result = await chat_service.get_chat_message_content(
        chat_history=history, settings=settings, kernel=kernel,
    )
    print(result.content)
    # [CALL] Math.calculate args={'expression': '3 ** 8'}
    # [DONE] Math.calculate result=6561 (1ms)
    # 3 to the power of 8 is 6561.

asyncio.run(main())
to invoke the actual function; omit it to short-circuit (skip the function entirely). Set
to override what the kernel sees as the function's output. Filters stack: if you add logging and safety filters, the logging filter runs first, calls
(which runs the safety filter), and the safety filter calls
to run the real function. This is the SK equivalent of middleware in a web framework.
and provide the deployment name, endpoint, and API key. Everything else — plugins, filters, memory, agents — works identically.

import asyncio, os
from semantic_kernel import Kernel
from semantic_kernel.connectors.ai.open_ai import AzureChatCompletion, AzureTextEmbedding

async def main():
    kernel = Kernel()

    # Azure OpenAI — uses AZURE_OPENAI_API_KEY, AZURE_OPENAI_ENDPOINT env vars by default
    kernel.add_service(
        AzureChatCompletion(
            service_id="chat",
            deployment_name="gpt-4o",            # your deployment name in Azure
            endpoint=os.environ["AZURE_OPENAI_ENDPOINT"],
            api_key=os.environ["AZURE_OPENAI_API_KEY"],
        )
    )

    # Azure embeddings for SemanticTextMemory
    kernel.add_service(
        AzureTextEmbedding(
            service_id="embedding",
            deployment_name="text-embedding-3-small",
            endpoint=os.environ["AZURE_OPENAI_ENDPOINT"],
            api_key=os.environ["AZURE_OPENAI_API_KEY"],
        )
    )

    # All plugins, memory, and agent patterns work unchanged
    kernel.add_plugin(MathPlugin(), plugin_name="Math")

    from semantic_kernel.connectors.ai.open_ai import OpenAIChatPromptExecutionSettings
    from semantic_kernel.connectors.ai.function_choice_behavior import FunctionChoiceBehavior
    from semantic_kernel.contents import ChatHistory

    settings = OpenAIChatPromptExecutionSettings(
        service_id="chat",
        function_choice_behavior=FunctionChoiceBehavior.Auto(),
    )
    history = ChatHistory()
    history.add_user_message("What is the square root of 144?")
    chat_service = kernel.get_service("chat")
    result = await chat_service.get_chat_message_content(
        chat_history=history, settings=settings, kernel=kernel,
    )
    print(result.content)  # The square root of 144 is 12.

asyncio.run(main())
are omitted, SK reads them from
environment variables. For managed identity authentication (no API key), pass an
instead. Azure AI Search integrates the same way: use
as the memory backend to give your agent access to enterprise document stores at scale.
Semantic Kernel centers on the Kernel: wire in an LLM service, register plugins, and invoke.

Define tools as @kernel_function-decorated methods on plugin classes. Use FunctionChoiceBehavior.Auto() for the automatic tool-calling loop. ChatCompletionAgent adds a stateful AgentThread for multi-turn conversations. SemanticTextMemory provides vector-backed retrieval augmentation with production connectors for Azure AI Search, Qdrant, and others. SK is the leading choice for Azure-based applications and multi-language teams.
